title: Variables & Data Types
overview: Every program works with data — numbers, text, true/false values. In this lecture you'll learn how to store data in variables and understand the different types of data Python can work with.

blocks.title: What is a Variable?
blocks.body: A **variable** is a named container that stores a value. Think of it like a labeled box where you can put information. You create a variable with the `=` sign.
bullets: Variables store values like numbers, text, or true/false
Use the = sign to assign a value: name = "Lakshanya"
Once assigned, use the variable name anywhere to access the value
Variables can be updated — old value is replaced

blocks.title: A Variable is a Labeled Jar
blocks.text: Imagine jars with labels. One jar labeled 'sugar' contains sugar. Another labeled 'flour' contains flour. You look at the label to know what's inside. In Python, the label is the variable name and the contents is the value.

blocks.title: Think Of A Variable As A Labeled Storage Box

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Labeled Storage Box
leftPanel.description: Each box has a label and holds one item

blocks.question: After writing name = 'Lakshanya', does the variable name store the word 'name' or the value 'Lakshanya'?
blocks.answer: The variable name stores the value 'Lakshanya'. Think of it like a box labeled 'name' that contains the item 'Lakshanya'. The label (variable name) helps you find the box, but the content is the actual value you stored.

leftPanel.label: Real Life
leftPanel.title: Labeled Storage
leftPanel.description: A box labeled 'age' stores the number 20 inside it.

blocks.title: Naming Rules
blocks.body: Variable names must follow clear rules.
bullets: Start with a letter or underscore (_), not a number
Use only letters, numbers, and underscores
Case-sensitive: myVar and myvar are different
Cannot use Python keywords like if, else, while, for, print

blocks.title: Creating Variables
blocks.code:
name = "Lakshanya"
age = 20
height = 5.6

print(name)
print(age)
print(height)

blocks.title: Updating Variables
blocks.code:
score = 0
print(score)

score = score + 10
print(score)

score = score + 5
print(score)

blocks.question: What would happen if you used a number as the first character of a variable name, like 1st_name = "John"?
blocks.answer: Python would show a SyntaxError because variable names cannot start with a number.

blocks.title: Store Information
blocks.instructions: Create three variables: your name, your age, and your college. Print each on a separate line.
blocks.starterCode:
# Store your information
name = ""
age = 
college = ""

# Print each variable
blocks.solution:
name = "Lakshanya"
age = 20
college = "SRM University"

print(name)
print(age)
print(college)
blocks.hint: Text values need quotes (" "), numbers don't. Use = to assign.

blocks.title: Every Value Has a Type
blocks.body: In Python, every value belongs to a **type** that tells Python what kind of data it is and what you can do with it.
bullets: int — whole numbers like 42, 0, -7
float — decimal numbers like 3.14, 2.5, -0.001
str — text/strings like "hello", "Python"
bool — True or False values
Use type() to check the type of any value

blocks.title: Think Of Data Types As Different Containers

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Different Containers
leftPanel.description: Each container type holds specific kinds of items

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
int → stores whole numbers (42)
float → stores decimals (3.14)
str → stores text ('Hello')
bool → stores True/False

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code:
age = 20
pi = 3.14
name = 'Alice'
is_student = True
print(age, pi, name, is_student)

blocks.question: Can you put text inside an int container? For example, int_name = 'Alice'.
blocks.answer: No! An int container only holds whole numbers. If you try age = 'Alice', Python will treat it as text (a string), not a number. The type matters because Python behaves differently with each type — you can do math with ints but not with strings.

blocks.title: Type Conversion
blocks.body: Sometimes you need to convert between types — turn a string into a number or a number into a string.
bullets: int("5") converts string "5" to integer 5
str(42) converts integer 42 to string "42"
float("3.14") converts string to float 3.14
Be careful: int("hello") causes an error because "hello" is not a number

blocks.title: Think Of Type Conversion As Repackaging

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Repackaging Items
leftPanel.description: Change the container type while preserving the value

blocks.question: If you have '42' (a string) and you call int('42'), does the original '42' string change permanently?
blocks.answer: No! Type conversion creates a new value in the new type. The original string '42' still exists as a string if you stored it in a variable. Python does not modify the original — it just converts it temporarily for the operation.

blocks.title: Type Conversion in Action
blocks.code:
age_str = "20"
print(age_str + "5")  # string concatenation

age_int = int(age_str)
print(age_int + 5)   # integer addition

pi = 3.14159
print("Pi is " + str(pi))

blocks.title: Common Type Errors
blocks.code:
# This causes an error:
# print("Age: " + 20)

# This works:
print("Age: " + str(20))

# So does this:
print(f"Age: {20}")

blocks.question: What do you think happens when you add string "10" and integer 10 together? Try: "10" + 10
blocks.answer: Python gives a TypeError. You cannot add a string and an integer directly. You must convert one to the other's type first using int() or str().

blocks.title: Age After 5 Years
blocks.instructions: Store your current age in a variable. Add 5 to it. Print the result using an f-string.
blocks.starterCode:
# Store current age
age = 

# Calculate age after 5 years
future_age = 

# Print the result
blocks.solution:
age = 20
future_age = age + 5
print(f"After 5 years, I will be {future_age} years old.")
blocks.hint: Use + to add numbers. Use f-strings with {} to insert variables into text.

blocks.title: Working with Strings
blocks.body: A **string** is a sequence of characters — letters, numbers, spaces, symbols — enclosed in quotes.
bullets: Use single quotes: 'Hello' or double quotes: "Hello"
Both work the same way — choose what you prefer
Strings can contain spaces, punctuation, and special characters
Add strings together with + (concatenation)

blocks.title: String Operations
blocks.code:
first = "Lakshanya"
last = "Krishna"

# Concatenation
full = first + " " + last
print(full)

# Repeat string
print("Ha! " * 3)

# Length
print(len(first))

blocks.title: Profile Card
blocks.instructions: Create variables for name, age, and college. Print them in this format: "Name: [name], Age: [age], College: [college]"
blocks.starterCode:
name = ""
age = 
college = ""

# Print profile card
blocks.solution:
name = "Lakshanya"
age = 20
college = "SRM University"

print("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + str(age) + ", College: " + college)
blocks.hint: Use + to join strings. Convert numbers to strings with str() before concatenating.

keyTakeaways.text: Variables store values using the = operator
int is for whole numbers, float is for decimals
str is for text, always in quotes
type() tells you the type of any value
Use int(), str(), float() to convert between types

whatYouLearned: How to create and update variables
The four basic data types: int, float, str, bool
How to check types with type()
How to convert between types safely
Why type errors happen and how to fix them
